coredata.dc:title: Water-soluble carbon dots with AIE characteristics for latent fingerprints recognition and information encryption
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

Liu, Fupeng
Shi, Qiang
Sun, Zhihai
Yang, Bing
Zhao, Ling
Cui, Fengzhe
Nie, Zhaogang
coredata.dc:description: As a promising category of nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered extensive attention. However, reports of simple, cost-effective, and eco-friendly method for synthesizing CDs with good water solubility and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics are scarce. Herein, water-soluble AIE-active CDs are obtained from common green tea via a facile solvothermal method, which is not only facile to operate but also makes full use of the natural resources in green tea, and shows excellent environmental protection and sustainable development concepts. The presence of functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino, on the surface of CDs confirms their good water solubility by characterizing the CDs. The experimental findings on the effects of solvent, viscosity, and concentration validate the AIE phenomena observed in CDs. These phenomena are attributed to the restraint of surface group motions (such as carboxyl and amino groups) and the reduction of the non-radiative decay rate in their aggregated state. Ultimately, the CDs are demonstrated to possess significant potential for applications in latent fingerprints recognition and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emission
Water solubility
Fingerprints recognition
Information encryption

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: To explore the optical properties of the CDs, UV\u2013Vis and PL spectra were measured and shown in Fig. 3. The UV\u2013Vis spectrum (Fig. 3a) shows an obvious absorption band around 270 nm, indicative of \u03C0\u2192\u03C0\u2217 transitions within the sp2-hybridized carbon core\u2014a characteristic feature also observed in other reported CDs [33]. In the PL spectra depicted in Fig. 3b, the CDs exhibit a red shift as the excitation wavelengths vary from 350 to 400 nm, demonstrating excitation-dependent behavior. When the excitation wavelength is 350 nm, the CDs emit bright blue light with a peak at 475 nm. Such an excitation-dependent PL is associated with the size distribution of CDs and/or the distribution of various emissive sites within the CDs [34,35]. To further evaluate their optical properties, we recorded the PL spectra in water/glycerol mixtures with different glycerol percentages (Fig. 3c) and in water/ethanol mixtures with different ethanol contents (Fig. 3d), all under excitation at 350 nm. Fig. 3c shows that the PL intensity of the CDs gradually enhances with the increase in glycerol percentage increases from 0 to 60 %. This indicates that augmenting the solvent viscosity can result in the PL enhancement of the CDs. It has been demonstrated that enhancing the solvent viscosity can limit the intramolecular motions of AIE molecules, thereby decreasing non-radiative transitions and leading to increased PL intensity [36]. This same effect was noted for other AIE-active CDs [37,38]. Consequently, the viscosity effect experiment implies that the CDs in question may exhibit AIE characteristics. Fig. 3d exhibits that the PL intensity of the CDs continues to increase with increasing the ethanol content from 0 to 60 %. The CDs are water-soluble but insoluble in ethanol. Therefore, ethanol, acting as a poor solvent, induces aggregation of the CDs. As evident from Fig. S1, upon reaching a 60 % ethanol concentration, the homogeneous solution transitions into a suspended and turbid state. The TEM images presented in Fig. 4a\u2013d further illustrate the impact of ethanol content on particle aggregation. This aggregation leads to the PL enhancement of the CDs, signifying that they exhibit typical AIE characteristics. To confirm that CDs exhibit the same AIE properties in various solvents, and considering that methanol is also a poor solvent for CDs, PL spectra of the CDs at different methanol concentrations were recorded (Fig. S2). The results demonstrated the universality of the CDs' AIE properties. Additionally, the results of the concentration effect experiment indicate that the PL intensity increases with the elevation of the CDs concentration, further supporting the AIE characteristics of CDs (Fig. S3).This work was supported by Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. ZR2018MA039 and ZR2021MA091), Guangdong Province Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2021A1515012049), the key research and development plan project of Shandong Province (Grant 2023CXPT083), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 11774071).

This work was supported by Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. ZR2018MA039 and ZR2021MA091 ), Guangdong Province Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2021A1515012049 ), the key research and development plan project of Shandong Province (Grant 2023CXPT083 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 11774071 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of AIE CDs.

(a) TEM image (upper inset: size distribution histogram; lower inset: high-resolution TEM), (b) XRD pattern, (c) Raman spectrum, (d) FTIR spectrum, (e) full XPS spectrum, and (f-h) high-resolution C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s XPS spectra of the CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorbance of the CDs; PL spectra of the CDs (1.6 mg mL−1) (b) at different excitation wavelengths, (c) in water/glycerol mixtures with different glycerol percentages, and (d) in water/ethanol mixtures with different ethanol contents, all under excitation at 350 nm.
TEM images of the CDs in water/ethanol mixtures with different ethanol contents.
FTIR spectra of the CDs (1.6 mg mL−1) in water/ethanol mixtures with different ethanol contents.
The fluorescent image of LFP on the glass slide under UV light (365 nm).

Photos of (a) the cartoon model and (b) the cartoon film in daylight. Fluorescence images of the cartoon film captured under 365 nm UV light: (c) one immediately after preparation and (d) the other after three months of storage.
Schematic illustration for information encryption by using CDs-PVA composites.

QY, τ, k
r , and k
nr of the CDs in water/ethanol mixtures with varying ethanol contents of 10 % and 60 %.

head.ce:title: Water-soluble carbon dots with AIE characteristics for latent fingerprints recognition and information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics Science and Information Technology, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics Science and Information Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics Science and Information Technology, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a promising category of nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered extensive attention. However, reports of simple, cost-effective, and eco-friendly method for synthesizing CDs with good water solubility and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics are scarce. Herein, water-soluble AIE-active CDs are obtained from common green tea via a facile solvothermal method, which is not only facile to operate but also makes full use of the natural resources in green tea, and shows excellent environmental protection and sustainable development concepts. The presence of functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino, on the surface of CDs confirms their good water solubility by characterizing the CDs. The experimental findings on the effects of solvent, viscosity, and concentration validate the AIE phenomena observed in CDs. These phenomena are attributed to the restraint of surface group motions (such as carboxyl and amino groups) and the reduction of the non-radiative decay rate in their aggregated state. Ultimately, the CDs are demonstrated to possess significant potential for applications in latent fingerprints recognition and information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the realm of nanotechnology, the development of novel materials with distinctive optical properties has markedly propelled advancements across multiple sectors, including biomedicine, environmental surveillance, and information security. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a particularly promising category of nanomaterials, owing to their exceptional optical properties, chemical stability, and biocompatibility [1–6]. However, most CDs are affected by the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon, resulting from excessive resonance energy transfer or direct π-π stacking between molecules [7]. This limitation hampers their utility at high concentrations or in the solid state. To tackle this issue, CDs are commonly dispersed within various matrix materials to create composites, including silica gel, starch, and inorganic salts [8–10]. Despite these efforts, the fundamental structural components of CDs persist unchanged, and the resultant composite materials continue to display the ACQ effect, which hampers the commercial viability of CDs-based materials [11].

In 2001, Tang and colleagues introduced the concept of aggregation-induced emission (AIE), a phenomenon that stands in stark contrast to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect [12,13]. Fluorescent molecules with AIE properties typically possess rotatable single bonds, which restrict intramolecular motions (such as vibration and rotation) in the aggregated state. This restriction reduces non-radiative transitions that would otherwise be induced by such motions, thereby resulting in enhanced fluorescence [14]. In 2013, Huang et al. initially reported the AIE-active fluorescent CDs, showcasing their promising applications in bioimaging and luminescent devices [15]. Subsequently, researchers have delved into and refined synthesis strategies for AIE CDs, successfully achieving effective modulation of their luminescence properties. Significant progress has been made in recent years in the research of AIE CDs [16]. For instance, Fu et al. [17] synthesized multi-colored AIE CDs using dithiosalicylic acid and ammonium sulfate as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. They explored the potential applications of these CDs in color-changing gels, anti-counterfeiting technology, and fingerprint detection. However, the CDs produced are expensive and environmentally harmful. Consequently, extensive research has been dedicated to developing biomass carbon sources and green synthesis methods for the preparation of AIE CDs. Jia et al. [18] reported the use of AIE CDs, which were prepared using abundantly accessible pitaya peel and 1,2-ethylenediamine as precursors, for the detection of tetracyclines in biological and food samples. They highlighted the eco-friendly and sustainable advantages of these CDs. These studies underscore the growing trend toward greener synthesis methods and the potential of biomass CDs for practical applications. However, to date, reports on the preparation of AIE CDs directly from undoped biomass carbon sources remain scarce, and the mechanism of AIE is still unclear, necessitating further in-depth study.

In this study, water-soluble, blue-emitting CDs are synthesized from green tea using a facile solvothermal technique. In previous reports, Khan [19] and Wen [20] also synthesized CDs utilizing tea as the carbon source via the hydrothermal method; however, their AIE characteristics were not investigated. Herein, the solvent effect, viscosity effect, and concentration effect experiments confirm the AIE characteristics of CDs. These characteristics arise from the suppression of surface carboxyl group motions and the reduction of non-radiative decay rate in their aggregated state. Owing to their water solubility and AIE characteristics, the synthesized CDs are employed for the recognition of latent fingerprints (LFPs) and information encryption, signifying their substantial potential for application within these domains.

Yipin green tea, selected as a representative green tea, was procured from a local supermarket in Liaocheng (Shandong, China). Dichloromethane and ethanol were supplied by Yantai Far East Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was acquired from the Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). A typical AIE material 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl) benzoic acid (TPE-COOH) was produced from Xi'an Qiyue Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanxi, China).

CDs were synthesized through a simple solvothermal process. The preparation procedure for CDs has been detailed in a previous publication [21]. Briefly, 4 g of tea powder was soaked in 100 mL of ethanol at room temperature. After 18 h, the tea extract was obtained by separating the upper layer of the clear liquid. 20 mL of the extract solution was mixed with 4 mL of deionized water, and then the mixture was heated in an autoclave at 160 °C for 3 h. The resultant solutions underwent centrifugation, subsequent filtration, and then extraction using a 1:1 vol ratio mixture of dichloromethane and water. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , the mixture was separated into two parts, where the top aqueous solution constituted the AIE CDs solutions. The AIE CDs powders were subsequently acquired through freeze-drying.

The morphology and dimensions of the prepared AIE CDs were characterized employing a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted using CuKα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm, 30 kV, 20 mA) to study the structure of the AIE CDs. Raman spectra were performed by means of an iHR550 grating spectrometer (iHR550, Horiba, Paris, France). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured using an irtracer-100 FTIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were carried out with a thermal ESCALAB 250XI multifunctional imaging electron spectrometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were acquired using a Hitachi U-3010 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were tested using an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer.

The morphology and structure of the prepared CDs were characterized using TEM, XRD and Raman spectroscopy. TEM studies demonstrate that the CDs are present in the form of uniformly distributed nanodots, as depicted in Fig. 2 a. The upper inset of Fig. 2a reveals that the size distribution conforms to a Gaussian function, featuring an average diameter of 2 nm and a full width at half-maximum of 0.58 nm, which suggests a relatively uniform size distribution. The high-resolution TEM image (lower inset of Fig. 2a) unveils the CDs' highly crystalline structure, exhibiting a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm that corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite [22]. The XRD pattern of the CDs (Fig. 2b) displays two pronounced, sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 26.9° and 40.8°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of graphene, respectively. This indicates that the CDs possess a high degree of graphitization and crystallinity. This is consistent with the XRD results of CDs reported by He et al. [23]. The Raman spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2c) exhibits two distinct peaks at 1345 cm−1 (D band) and 1585 cm−1 (G band), signifying the presence of both disordered and graphitic carbon structures within the CDs [24]. The intensity ratio of the G band to the D band (IG/ID) stands at 1.50, indicating a high degree of graphitization and crystallinity in the CDs [25]. These findings agree with the results obtained from XRD and TEM analyses.

The surface functional groups and chemical composition of the CDs were analyzed using FTIR and XPS spectra. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2d), the CDs show obvious vibrational absorptions of O-H and N-H functionalities ranging from 3540 to 3100 cm−1, C-H at 2984 cm−1, -COOH at 1642 cm−1, C=C and C-N between 1490 and 1280 cm−1, and C-O at 1043 cm−1, signifying the presence of diverse functional groups on the surfaces of CDs [26–28]. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 2e) exhibits three distinct peaks at binding energies of 284.9, 400.2, and 532.1 eV, indicating that the CDs are mainly composed of C (71.96 %), N (2.46 %), and O (25.59 %) elements. Fig. 2f illustrates the high-resolution C1s spectra of the CDs, which can be deconvoluted into three components: C-C/C=C (284.5 eV), C-O/C-N (285.9 eV), and C=O groups (287.8 eV) [29]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) can be fitted into three peaks at 531.1, 532.6, and 535.8 eV, corresponding to C=O, C-O, and adsorbed water, respectively [30]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2h) is resolved into two peaks at 399.4 eV (amino N) and 400.1 eV (pyrrole N) [31]. Both FTIR and XPS results confirm the presence of various functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino, on the surface of the CDs, thereby affirming their good water solubility [32].

To explore the optical properties of the CDs, UV–Vis and PL spectra were measured and shown in Fig. 3 . The UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 3a) shows an obvious absorption band around 270 nm, indicative of π→π∗ transitions within the sp2-hybridized carbon core—a characteristic feature also observed in other reported CDs [33]. In the PL spectra depicted in Fig. 3b, the CDs exhibit a red shift as the excitation wavelengths vary from 350 to 400 nm, demonstrating excitation-dependent behavior. When the excitation wavelength is 350 nm, the CDs emit bright blue light with a peak at 475 nm. Such an excitation-dependent PL is associated with the size distribution of CDs and/or the distribution of various emissive sites within the CDs [34,35]. To further evaluate their optical properties, we recorded the PL spectra in water/glycerol mixtures with different glycerol percentages (Fig. 3c) and in water/ethanol mixtures with different ethanol contents (Fig. 3d), all under excitation at 350 nm. Fig. 3c shows that the PL intensity of the CDs gradually enhances with the increase in glycerol percentage increases from 0 to 60 %. This indicates that augmenting the solvent viscosity can result in the PL enhancement of the CDs. It has been demonstrated that enhancing the solvent viscosity can limit the intramolecular motions of AIE molecules, thereby decreasing non-radiative transitions and leading to increased PL intensity [36]. This same effect was noted for other AIE-active CDs [37,38]. Consequently, the viscosity effect experiment implies that the CDs in question may exhibit AIE characteristics. Fig. 3d exhibits that the PL intensity of the CDs continues to increase with increasing the ethanol content from 0 to 60 %. The CDs are water-soluble but insoluble in ethanol. Therefore, ethanol, acting as a poor solvent, induces aggregation of the CDs. As evident from Fig. S1, upon reaching a 60 % ethanol concentration, the homogeneous solution transitions into a suspended and turbid state. The TEM images presented in Fig. 4 a–d further illustrate the impact of ethanol content on particle aggregation. This aggregation leads to the PL enhancement of the CDs, signifying that they exhibit typical AIE characteristics. To confirm that CDs exhibit the same AIE properties in various solvents, and considering that methanol is also a poor solvent for CDs, PL spectra of the CDs at different methanol concentrations were recorded (Fig. S2). The results demonstrated the universality of the CDs' AIE properties. Additionally, the results of the concentration effect experiment indicate that the PL intensity increases with the elevation of the CDs concentration, further supporting the AIE characteristics of CDs (Fig. S3).

The AIE characteristics of the CDs were further investigated through the assessment of their PL quantum yields (QY) and decay lifetimes (τ) in water/ethanol mixtures with varying ethanol contents of 10 % and 60 % (Fig. S4). The radiative rate constants (k
r ), and non-radiative rate constants (k
nr ) were derived utilizing Eqs. (1) and (2),
(1)
k r
= QY / τ
(2)
k nr
=
(
1 − QY
)
/ τ
respectively [39]. Their corresponding values are presented in Table 1 . The QY of sample A (10 %) was found to be a mere 0.04 %, which is substantially lower than that of sample B (60 %), which stands at 2.65 %. Furthermore, sample B exhibits a higher k
r value and a significantly lower k
nr compared to sample A. Consequently, we can deduce that the aggregation significantly diminishes the non-radiative decay rate, thereby leading to a substantial enhancement in the PL efficiency of the CDs [39].

Based on previous reports, the AIE process may potentially be linked to mechanisms including intramolecular motion restriction and the formation of J-aggregates [40]. In general, the occurrence of J-aggregation leads to significant red shifts in the absorbance and PL spectra [41]. To verify the formation of J-aggregates within the CDs, UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the CDs in water/ethanol mixtures with different ethanol contents were recorded and presented in Fig. S5. As evident from the graph, there are no obvious shifts in the absorption peaks, suggesting that as the ethanol content increases from 0 to 80 %, the formation of J-aggregates does not occur. Additionally, when examining Fig. 3d, the PL spectra of the CDs also do not exhibit a significant red shift with increasing ethanol concentration, further confirming that the formation of J-aggregates does not take place. To disclose the origin of AIE behaviors, FTIR spectra of the CDs in water/ethanol mixtures with different ethanol contents were measured, as shown in Fig. 5 . As the ethanol content is increased from 0 to 80 %, a gradual decline in the stretching vibration intensities of both -COOH and N-H/O-H is evident, indicating the suppression of surface group motions. Similar phenomena were also observed in other CDs [26,42]. If the restriction of surface group motions is the dominant factor, lower temperatures should further constrain their motions, thereby enhancing fluorescence [43]. To further validate the proposed mechanism that PL enhancement arises from surface group motions restriction, PL spectra of the CDs at different temperatures were measured (Fig. S6). The results demonstrate a significant enhancement in PL intensity with decreasing temperature, thus confirming this mechanism. Given the aforementioned analysis, the augmented PL efficiency observed in the aggregated state can be ascribed to the restricted motion of surface groups, specifically those of carboxyl and amino groups, as well as the reduction of non-radiative transition.

Given their good water solubility and AIE characteristics, the synthesized CDs are well dispersed in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to obtain CDs-PVA composites for LFP recognition and information encryption. Fig. S7 presents a schematic illustration for the nondestructive collection and recognition of a LFP by using CDs-PVA composites. An appropriate amount of CDs-PVA aqueous solution is dropped onto a substrate that has been previously coated with a LFP. Following the evaporation of the solvent, the resultant flexible and transparent film is carefully peeled away from the substrate. When illuminated with 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) light, the distinct features of the fingerprint become clearly discernible in the LFP image. In this work, CDs-PVA flexible films, rather than CDs powders, are utilized for the collection of LFPs. This choice is primarily driven by the fact that films offer superior clarity, safety, and compatibility. Moreover, they possess the significant advantage of being easy to carry and able to store for several years, facilitating further analyses [26,44]. Usually, fingerprints are analyzed at three levels of detail to identify individual identity. Level 1 features refer to the overall characteristics of fingerprints, including their patterns, core points, and delta points. Level 2 details represent the macroscopic features of the fingerprint ridges, typically including bifurcations, bridges, short ridges, and so forth. Level 3 features encompass the microscopic details of fingerprints, such as the shape of the ridge edges and the width of the ridges and pores. Level 2 and Level 3 features are frequently utilized for individual identification due to their unique characteristics [45]. As depicted in Fig. 6 , under 365 nm UV light, the LFP film obtained from a glass slide exhibits an arch-typed fingerprint pattern (Level 1). In the magnified images (Fig. 6a–f), six feature points can be clearly observed: bifurcation (Level 2), bridge (Level 2), short ridge (Level 2), pore (Level 3), termination (Level 2), and crossover (Level 2) [46], all of which are discernible due to the high contrast in fluorescence signals. Additionally, LFPs are also successfully detected on various common substrates, including ceramics, plastics, and stainless steel (Fig. S8). These findings suggest their potential application for LFPs recognition.

To investigate its underlying mechanism, we employ a cartoon model with a patterned relief structure (Fig. 7 a), analogous to the structure of a fingerprint on a substrate. This model is then utilized to obtain the cartoon film (Fig. 7b) through preparation process identical to that utilized for a LFP film. Under 365 nm UV light, the fluorescent cartoon character is successfully depicted on the film (Fig. 7c). After three months, there is no discernible change in this image (Fig. 7d). Furthermore, after sixteen months, the cartoon character remains clearly visible (Fig. S9). Additionally, to further illustrate the fluorescent stability of the films, PL spectra of different areas in the cartoon film before and after long-term (sixteen months) storage are tested (Fig. S9). The results indicate that the cartoon character, even after 16 months, still exhibits significant fluorescence and forms a clear contrast with the dark area, allowing the pattern of the cartoon character to be clearly rendered under 365 nm UV light. These observations align well with the LFP image findings. Therefore, we suggest that during the solvent evaporation process, the coffee ring effect, arising from the outward capillary flow, leads to varied spatial distributions of CDs within the polymer film. Specifically, CDs tend to migrate towards the peripheries of the relief sections/fingerprint ridges, yielding an enhanced PL intensity. This is consistent with the analysis results presented by Chen et al. [44]. In addition, the viscosity effect of lipid secretions present in fingerprints can restrict intramolecular motion within AIE CDs, thereby enhancing fluorescence [45].

A model consisting of six letters is selected to demonstrate the potential applications of CDs in information encryption. As depicted in Fig. 8 , the letters A, C, and E are written using a marker pen containing the CDs-PVA aqueous solution, whereas B, D, and F are penned with a marker pen filled with a commercially available TPE-COOH ethanol solution. TPE-COOH is a typical AIE material that exhibits blue emission under 365 nm UV light, with ethanol as a good solvent and water as a poor solvent. Therefore, under the excitation of 365 nm, the model displays blue “ABCDEF”. After spraying water, the letters A, C, and E disappear due to good water solubility of the CDs, and the wrong message “BDF” displays. The correct information “ACE” will only be displayed after spraying alcohol. This indicates that the CDs have great potential in the application of multiple information encryption.

In summary, the CDs that possess good water solubility and AIE characteristics are successfully synthesized via a low-cost, eco-friendly, and facile solvothermal method. TEM, XRD, and Raman spectroscopy are utilized to verify the superior graphitization and crystallinity properties of the CDs. The FTIR and XPS analyses indicate that the CDs primarily consist of C, N, and O, and exhibit good water solubility due to the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino functional groups on their surfaces. The CDs exhibit an excitation-dependent PL behavior associated with the size distribution of CDs and/or the distribution of various emissive sites within the CDs. Additionally, the solvent effect, viscosity effect, and concentration effect experiments confirm the AIE characteristics of CDs, which originate from the suppression of surface group motions, particularly those involving carboxyl and amino groups, coupled with a decrease of the non-radiative decay rate in their aggregated state. Given their good water solubility and AIE characteristics, the CDs have great potential applications in LFP recognition and information encryption.

Yingwei Zhou: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Fupeng Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Qiang Shi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization. Zhihai Sun: Resources, Conceptualization. Bing Yang: Formal analysis. Ling Zhao: Formal analysis. Fengzhe Cui: Formal analysis. Zhaogang Nie: Validation, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. ZR2018MA039 and ZR2021MA091), Guangdong Province Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2021A1515012049), the key research and development plan project of Shandong Province (Grant 2023CXPT083), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 11774071).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Progress and obstacles in employing carbon quantum dots for sustainable wastewater treatment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Khan, S. Shukla, M. Kumar, D. Barcelo, A. Zuorro, P.C. Bhargava, Progress and obstacles in employing carbon quantum dots for sustainable wastewater treatment, Environ. Res. 261 (2024) 119671, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envres.2024.119671.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in photo-responsive carbon dots for tumor therapy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Algae derived carbon dots and its polymeric composites for white light emission

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in highly luminescent carbon dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eco-friendly synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots for visual alcohol detection and light-emitting diodes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State-of-the-art of biomass-derived carbon dots: preparation, properties, and applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon‐based quantum dots with solid‐state photoluminescent: mechanism, implementation, and application
